docs.title: What Is Climbo?
docs.text:
One-line summary: Climbo is a white-label AI reputation management platform you resell to local businesses under your own brand.

tags: [getting-started, overview] sources: [BRocA7husPU, sIGOLZ1kFAQ] last_updated: 2026-07-11

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Reputation management is not just getting Google reviews. Here is what it actually covers:

  1. Collect reviews — get more positive reviews flowing consistently
  2. Protect the rating — give unhappy customers a private feedback channel so they can be heard before posting publicly
  3. Reply to every review — with AI-generated, keyword-rich responses
  4. Market with reviews — share five-star reviews on social media and your website
  5. Keep listings accurate — correct hours, photos, contact info on Google
  6. Get found on AI search — make sure ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI answers mention the business

Climbo automates all six of these. You set it up once. The system runs from there.

docs.title: Why local businesses need this
docs.text:
The data is clear:

  • 98% of customers read reviews before calling a local business (BrightLocal)
  • Increasing a rating by just 0.5 stars leads to 59% more revenue (Harvard Business Review)
  • Getting into the Google Maps top-3 results generates 126% more traffic (BrightLocal)
  • Five-star profiles get 35% more calls than four-star profiles (Search Engine Land)

A dentist with 24 reviews while the competitor two blocks away has 350 reviews is losing patients every day. That dentist is your target client. They understand the problem immediately when you show them the comparison.

docs.title: What Climbo is not
docs.text:
• It is not a service you deliver manually. You set up automation; the software does the work.
  • It is not visible to your clients as "Climbo." They see your brand name and logo.
  • It is not a lead generation tool. It keeps businesses found once they have customers. Your job is to bring in the business owner as a client; their job is to serve their own customers.
  • It is not a quick-rich scheme. Giacomo says this explicitly: it is a real business that requires consistent sales effort.

docs.title: The agency model in plain terms
docs.text:
You pay Climbo: €99/month (unlimited clients, all features).

You charge your clients: anywhere from $100 to $500+ per month depending on the package.

Your clients never know Climbo exists. They log into your branded platform at your custom domain. They see your AI assistant name. Every email they receive comes from your domain.

You are the agency. Climbo is your back-end.

docs.title: Key details / Tips
docs.text:
• Start by targeting businesses with low review counts, stale ratings, and active competitors beating them on Google — they feel the pain immediately
  • The free trial strategy ("let me get you 5 reviews for free, no credit card") consistently converts — once a business owner sees reviews coming in, the sale is done
  • Clients stay because the system keeps working. Churn comes from bad onboarding, not from the product failing.

docs.title: Onboarding Clients
docs.text:
One-line summary: Step-by-step setup after a client says yes — takes about 30 minutes and sets the tone for the whole relationship.

tags: [operations, onboarding, clients] sources: [skool-onboarding-is-not-optional, skool-first-client-checklist, BRocA7husPU] last_updated: 2026-07-11

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Onboarding is not optional. A community post with 26 comments said it best: "The better you get your clients set up, the longer they stay." Every minute you invest in a proper first session pays back in lower churn and fewer support questions later.

Plan for one 30-minute screen share or in-person session with the client. You do most of the setup; they watch and confirm details.

docs.title: What you need from the client before you start
docs.text:
• Google Business Profile access: Either they add you as a Manager on their GBP, or they share their Google login with you temporarily. Manager access is preferred — go to GBP > Settings > Managers > Add.
  • Business logo (PNG, transparent background preferred)
  • Customer list: A CSV or spreadsheet with past customers (name + phone and/or email). Even 20-30 names is enough to start.
  • Preferred review request message: A short message in their voice — you can draft it, they approve.

docs.title: Step-by-step setup
docs.text:
Step 1 — Create the client in Climbo Go to your Climbo dashboard > Clients > New Client. Enter business name, address, and contact email.

Step 2 — Connect their Google Business Profile (GBP) In the client's Climbo account, go to Integrations > Google. Authorize using their GBP credentials. Verify the correct location is linked (important for multi-location businesses).

Step 3 — Set up the review request template Go to Settings > Review Requests. Customize the SMS and/or email template with the business name. Keep it short and personal. Example: "Hi [name], thanks for visiting [Business]. We'd love your feedback — it takes 30 seconds: [link]"

Step 4 — Configure the AI agents - Enable the SEO Agent (always) - Enable the Social Agent (always) - Enable the GEO Agent (optional at start — include it if the client is on a higher plan) If you've white-labeled the AI, rename the agents in the client's view.

Step 5 — Import the past customer list Go to Contacts > Import. Upload the CSV. Climbo accepts name, phone, and/or email. The import limit is 100 per batch — for larger lists, do multiple batches.

Step 6 — Send the first reactivation batch Start with 10–20 contacts. Send the review request manually so you can see what goes out. Check that links work. This is also your proof-of-concept: reviews coming in within 24 hours is a strong wow moment.

Step 7 — Show the client the dashboard Walk them through: where reviews appear, what the agents are doing, how to check their rating. Set expectations: "You'll start getting reviews within a few days. Month 1 goal is 10 new reviews."

Step 8 — Month 1 goal Aim for 10+ new reviews in the first 30 days. This is achievable from a reactivation campaign with a modest customer list. Hitting this target early builds client confidence and retention.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Skipping GBP connection — without it, the SEO and GEO agents don't work and review links don't point to the right profile
  • Importing cold contacts — only use real past customers (see [[compliance/gdpr-eu]])
  • Sending the full list at once — start small so you can review quality and timing
  • Not setting expectations — clients who expect 100 reviews in a week will churn at 20

docs.title: Key details / Tips
docs.text:
• Total setup time: ~30 minutes with a cooperative client
  • Do the setup yourself rather than sending instructions — clients who set it up alone are slower to get results and more likely to cancel
  • The first batch of reviews is your best sales material: screenshot them and send to the client with a message like "Look what came in this week"

docs.title: Client Retention
docs.text:
One-line summary: How to keep clients paying month after month — reports, wow moments, upsells, and warning signs.

tags: [operations, retention, churn] sources: [skool-onboarding-is-not-optional, BRocA7husPU, skool-community-feed] last_updated: 2026-07-11

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A client who signed up and got onboarded is not a retained client yet. Retention happens when the client sees ongoing value, month after month. Most churn in reputation management happens in months 2-3, when the excitement of the first reviews fades.

Your job is to keep the value visible.

docs.title: Monthly report
docs.text:
Send a monthly report to every client. It does not need to be complex. At minimum include:

  • Reviews gained this month (count and examples)
  • Average rating change (before vs. now)
  • AI activity summary (what the SEO and Social agents published)

Climbo has a printable report feature (released Mar 2026 — available in the client's dashboard). Use it. A PDF sent by email or WhatsApp on the same day each month signals professionalism.

If you use the [[features/google-sheets]] integration, share a live Google Sheet with the client so they can check their numbers anytime.

docs.title: The "wow moment" delivery
docs.text:
Do not wait for the monthly report to share good news. When a 5-star review comes in, screenshot it and send it directly to the client — WhatsApp is fine. The message is simple:

"This came in this week for you."

One sentence. One screenshot. This is the most retention-effective thing you can do. It makes the invisible work visible in real time and it reminds the client why they are paying you.

docs.title: Upsell path
docs.text:
Start clients on the minimum and expand:

  1. Review requests only — the base product
  2. Add Social Agent — automated posts from their reviews
  3. Add GEO Agent — AI search optimization (ChatGPT, Gemini citations)
  4. Add Mobile App — white-labeled app for the client
  5. Add NFC cards — physical review collection at the counter
  6. Upgrade to annual plan — offered at a discount, locks in the relationship

Do not try to sell everything at once. Get them to see value from step 1, then introduce step 2 after a month.

docs.title: Warning signs a client is about to churn
docs.text:
• They stop opening your monthly reports (check email open rates if your tool allows it)
  • They ask "how many reviews did I get?" instead of checking the dashboard — means they don't feel connected to the product
  • They complain the pace is "too slow" — usually means you haven't reset expectations, or the reactivation campaign ran out of contacts
  • They ask about pausing — treat this as a churn signal, not an administrative request; book a call immediately

docs.title: What to say when churn signs appear
docs.text: Book a 15-minute call. Show them their progress chart. Reframe pace: "You've gone from 12 reviews to 34 in 90 days. That's a 3x increase. Google's algorithm responds to sustained growth, not spikes." Then propose the next step (add Social Agent, add NFC cards, import a new batch of customers).

docs.title: Key details / Tips
docs.text:
• The Skool community post "Onboarding is not optional" (26 comments) is essentially about retention — clients who get a proper setup stay longer
  • Month 1 target is 10 reviews; month 3 target is 30 total; by month 6 most clients won't consider cancelling
  • Referrals from retained clients are your best source of new clients — one Skool member shared that a long-term client brought a friend to a lunch meeting, which turned into a new client on the spot

docs.title: SMTP Setup
docs.text:
One-line summary: Connect your own email domain to Climbo so review request emails come from @yourdomain.com instead of a generic address.

tags: [operations, smtp, email, white-label] sources: [HZ-9avetOnE] last_updated: 2026-07-11

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
By default, Climbo sends review request emails using its own mail server. This works, but the "from" address will not be your brand — it will be a Climbo address.

SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) lets you connect your own email account so that emails sent by Climbo appear to come from you. Example: instead of noreply@climbo.com, your clients' customers receive a message from reviews@youragency.com or hello@yourbusiness.it.

This feature was announced in the AMA of June 25 2026.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
• Trust: Emails from a recognized domain get opened more. Emails from an unknown generic address get ignored or marked as spam.
  • Deliverability: Properly configured domains with SPF/DKIM records have lower spam rates than shared mail servers.
  • Professionalism: If you're white-labeling Climbo under your agency brand, everything — including emails — should look like it comes from you.

docs.title: How to set it up
docs.text:
1. Go to your Climbo dashboard
  2. Navigate to Settings > Email > SMTP
  3. Enter your SMTP details:
  4. Host: e.g. smtp.gmail.com or smtp-relay.brevo.com
  5. Port: 587 (TLS) or 465 (SSL)
  6. Username: your email address
  7. Password: your SMTP password or app password
  8. Send a test email to confirm it works

docs.title: Recommended SMTP providers
docs.text:
Provider Cost Notes Google Workspace ~€6/month per user Reliable, familiar, good deliverability Brevo (formerly Sendinblue) Free up to 300 emails/day Good free tier for starting out Zoho Mail Free plan available Budget option, lower limits

For most agency owners starting out, Brevo's free tier covers review request volumes for the first several months. Upgrade to Google Workspace when you want the polished @yourdomain.com identity.

docs.title: Key details / Tips
docs.text:
• If you use Gmail (personal), you need to generate an App Password in your Google account security settings — the regular password will not work
  • Make sure your domain has SPF and DKIM records set correctly, or your emails may still end up in spam even with SMTP connected
  • The SMTP setting applies to all review request emails across your Climbo account
  • This is also where the Email History feature lives — you can review what was sent and when

docs.title: Demo Script — Pitching to a Local Business
docs.text:
One-line summary: You are selling to a local business owner (restaurant, dentist, salon, etc.), not to another agency. Your pitch is about their growth on Google, not about yours.

tags: [sales, demo, pitch, local-business] sources: [BRocA7husPU, skool-posts, PLAYBOOK] last_updated: 2026-07-11

docs.title: The frame
docs.text:
You are a marketing agency that helps local businesses get more Google reviews, rank higher on Maps, and be found by AI assistants like ChatGPT and Google AI Overviews.

You are not selling software. You are selling a result: more visibility, more clients, more revenue.

The tool you use under the hood (Climbo, white-labeled as your brand) is invisible to the business owner. They see your brand, not Climbo's.

docs.title: The 60-second cold open (face-to-face)
docs.text:
Walk in, introduce yourself, then:

"Hi, I work with local businesses in [city] to help them rank higher on Google. Quick question — when someone searches '[their service] in [city]', do you know where you show up?

[Pull out phone, show Google Maps results]

Your competitor [Name] has 280 reviews. You have 34. Google's algorithm treats that as a signal of trust. That's why they're above you.

I can get you your next 5 reviews this week, at zero cost to you. No contract, no commitment. If you see results, we can talk about continuing. Sound fair?"

You are offering proof before payment. The free trial is your close.

docs.title: The 10-minute demo (if they want to see more)
docs.text: Once they say yes to the free trial, schedule a 10-minute onboarding:

docs.title: Step 1 — Show the problem (2 min)
docs.text:
• Open Google Maps, show their profile vs. the top competitor
  • Show their last review date — if it was 6 months ago, their velocity is dead
  • "Google sees no activity. That tells the algorithm you're less relevant."

docs.title: Step 2 — Show the solution (5 min)
docs.text:
• "I use a system that sends a simple text to your past customers. They tap a link, they leave a review. No app, no account needed."
  • Demo the NFC card if you have one: "Or — this. A customer taps here with their phone and they're on your review page in 2 seconds."
  • Show them the review request interface (use a demo client account)

docs.title: Step 3 — Show the bigger picture (3 min)
docs.text:
• "Every review we generate: our AI writes a reply optimized for Google search. That reply contains the keywords people search for — 'best [service] in [city]'."
  • "The same review gets turned into a social post for your Google Business page automatically. More activity = higher ranking."
  • "And once you have enough content, you start appearing in AI search — ChatGPT, Google AI Overview. When someone asks 'best dentist in Formia', your name comes up."

docs.title: The offer
docs.text:
Once the free trial gets them 3–5 reviews:

"You've seen it works. Here's how we continue: for €[price]/month I manage everything — review requests, AI replies, social posts, and your Google ranking. You don't touch anything. You just get more clients."

Keep pricing simple. One monthly fee. No setup costs visible to them (you absorb those). The value you sell is the result, not the features.

docs.title: Key details / Tips
docs.text:
• Never lead with "software." Business owners don't care about software. They care about customers.
  • The competitor angle is the most powerful hook. Show the gap, not the product.
  • NFC card = physical wow moment. Hand it to them, let them tap their own phone. The review page opens in 2 seconds. That's the demo.
  • Free trial = lowest resistance entry. You pay the balance costs (few cents per SMS) for the trial. It's the cost of acquiring a client.
  • Keep the AI framing simple: "It automatically writes replies to your reviews and posts them on your Google page." Don't say "SEO agent" or "GEO agent."
  • Track the outcome, not the process. Monthly report: "You got 23 new reviews this month. You moved from position 8 to position 4 on Google Maps."

docs.title: What you are NOT selling
docs.text:
• "A $3k/mo reputation management business" — that's the pitch Climbo uses to recruit agency owners like you. It has nothing to do with your client's reality.
  • AI software, SaaS, dashboards, or technology
  • A "white-label platform"

You are selling: more Google reviews → higher ranking → more phone calls → more revenue. Everything else is behind the curtain.

docs.title: Finding Clients
docs.text:
One-line summary: Your ideal client is a local business with few reviews, a competitor beating them on Google, and an owner who already values online marketing.

tags: [sales, prospecting, client-acquisition] sources: [BRocA7husPU, SKOOL-ANALYSIS, skool-simple-yet-effective] last_updated: 2026-07-11

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The best clients for reputation management share four characteristics. Finding them is straightforward once you know where to look. Giacomo's training uses the phrase "foot in the door" — your goal is not to sell immediately but to prove the system works first.

docs.title: The perfect target profile
docs.text:
Look for local businesses that have: 1. Low review count — fewer than 50 Google reviews is ideal; under 20 is excellent 2. Low or stagnant rating — 3.5–4.3 stars (not 4.8+ where the sale is harder) 3. A competitor beating them — the competitor in the next street has 5× more reviews 4. Marketing mindset already — they have a website, post on Instagram, have claimed their GBP

Avoid businesses that: - Have never heard of Google reviews and show zero interest in digital marketing - Already have 500+ reviews and a 4.9 rating (they don't feel the pain) - Are too small to pay $100/month (micro-businesses with one employee)

docs.title: Method 1 — Google Maps research
docs.text:
Search [service] in [your city] on Google Maps. Look at the results on pages 2–3. These businesses are already trying to be found but are losing to competitors.

For each one, note: - Review count - Rating - Last review date (if it was 6 months ago, their velocity is dead) - Competitor's review count for comparison

Build a list of 30 targets per month.

docs.title: Method 2 — Local Facebook groups
docs.text:
One community member got 4 clients this way (38-comment Skool post):

"1) Find a local business Facebook group for your area. 2) Post saying you've created a solution for local businesses that will help them grow on Google through the power of reviews, on autopilot with AI. 3) Offer all businesses that commented 14 days for free including a review reactivation where you cover the costs to send a campaign to their past customer list. 4) Around 10 businesses commented."

docs.title: Method 3 — Face-to-face visits
docs.text:
The highest-conversion method, especially for first clients. Giacomo recommends visiting one local business per day:

"Visit one local business per day. Ask for 60 seconds to show their Google ranking versus their competitor."

You walk in, show them on your phone that their competitor has 300 reviews and they have 20, and ask: "Would you like me to get you 5 free reviews this week?" If yes, you have a free trial. No commitment for them.

docs.title: Method 4 — Climbo Prospecting Tool
docs.text: Climbo has a built-in prospecting tool that identifies local businesses by category with low review counts. Check your agency dashboard for this feature.

docs.title: Method 5 — Competitor's clients
docs.text: From the community (May 2026): a member landed a client from BirdEye. "Every business currently on BirdEye can level up and join Climbo." If you know a business is on a competitor tool, they already understand the value. You just need to show why yours is better.

docs.title: The outreach script
docs.text:
Face-to-face cold visit:

"Hi, I'm [Name]. Quick question — did you know that when someone searches '[service] in [city]', Google is showing [Competitor] above you because of their review count? I can show you in 60 seconds. I'd like to offer to get you your next 5 reviews completely free this week. No commitment."

You are not selling yet. You are offering proof.

docs.title: Key details / Tips
docs.text:
• Niche focus works. One community member focused on restaurants for 2.5 months with no results, then closed 5 restaurants on annual plans in one week — €3,000. Persistence in a niche pays off.
  • Industries that respond best: restaurants, dentists, beauty salons, gyms, law firms, plumbers, contractors — any business with direct customer contact and reputation at stake.
  • The free trial converts. Once the owner's phone buzzes with "New 5-star review," the paid sale is effectively closed.
  • Tuesday–Thursday are best for cold visits. Mondays owners are swamped; Fridays they leave early.
